title: Passive Voice — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Full passive across all tenses + reporting passives + causative — IELTS Task 1 essential
intro: Passive voice is critical for IELTS Writing Task 1 (process diagrams are 70% passive) and high-impact for Task 2 academic register. Band 7+ candidates control passive in ALL 12 tenses, plus reporting passives ('It is said that…') and causative structures ('have something done'). This reference covers the full system.

sections.title: 1. Active vs Passive — When and Why
sections.summary: Passive shifts focus from the doer to the receiver of the action. Use it deliberately, not by default.

examples.singular: Active: They built the bridge in 1850.
examples.plural: Passive: The bridge was built in 1850.

examples.singular: Active: Scientists have discovered a new species.
examples.plural: Passive: A new species has been discovered.

examples.singular: Active: The committee will announce the results tomorrow.
examples.plural: Passive: The results will be announced tomorrow.

rules.note: Formula: Active O → Passive S. Active V → 'be' + V3. Active S → 'by + agent' (often dropped).

items: 1. The doer is unknown: 'My bike was stolen yesterday.' (don't know who)
2. The doer is unimportant: 'The school was built in 1980.' (we don't care who built it)
3. The doer is obvious: 'The criminal was arrested.' (obviously by police)
4. To put the receiver in focus: 'The economy is being affected by inflation.' (focus on economy, not inflation)
When the doer is the most important information
When the active sounds clearer and more direct
Throughout an entire essay (overuse sounds vague and bureaucratic)
rules.note: Band 6 candidates use almost no passive. Band 6.5 over-use it ('It can be said that it has been argued that it is believed that…'). Band 7+ uses passive deliberately, mixed with active.

sections.title: 2. Passive Across All Tenses
sections.summary: Memorise the passive form in every tense. The verb 'build' is conjugated below for reference.

examples.plural: is/are built — Houses ARE BUILT every year.
is/are being built — A new bridge IS BEING BUILT.
has/have been built — Several schools HAVE BEEN BUILT recently.
has/have been being built — Mostly avoided; rephrase as Present Perfect.
was/were built — The bridge WAS BUILT in 1850.
was/were being built — While the road WAS BEING BUILT, traffic was diverted.
had been built — By 1900, the cathedral HAD BEEN BUILT.
had been being built — Avoided; rephrase.
will be built — A new airport WILL BE BUILT next year.
will be being built — Avoided; rephrase as 'will be under construction'.
will have been built — By 2030, the system WILL HAVE BEEN BUILT.
is/are going to be built — A new hospital IS GOING TO BE BUILT.

examples.singular: must / should / can / may + be + V3
examples.plural: The document MUST BE SIGNED. / Schools SHOULD BE FUNDED. / The work CAN BE DONE quickly. / Permission MAY BE GRANTED.
must/should/could/might HAVE BEEN + V3 — The decision SHOULD HAVE BEEN MADE earlier. / The accident COULD HAVE BEEN PREVENTED.

rules.note: Modal passive is high-yield for Band 7+ — 'must be addressed', 'should be implemented', 'can be achieved', 'might have been prevented'. Drop one in every Task 2 essay.

sections.tip: Conjugate 'build' through all 12 tenses in passive on one page. Then do the same for 'introduce', 'discover', 'recognise' — three high-frequency academic verbs.

sections.title: 3. Passive of Reporting Verbs — 'It Is Said That…'
sections.summary: Two passive structures for reporting. Both make Task 2 essays sound objective and academic.

rules.pattern: TYPE 1: It + passive reporting verb + that-clause

examples.plural: It is said that smoking causes cancer.
It is widely believed that climate change is human-induced.
It is estimated that 1 billion people lack clean water.
It has been argued that technology has worsened isolation.
It is expected that the population will reach 9 billion by 2050.

rules.pattern: TYPE 2: Subject + passive reporting verb + to-infinitive

examples.plural: Smoking is said to cause cancer.
He is believed to be hiding in a remote location.
He is believed to have stolen the money. (past = to have + V3)
The economy is expected to recover by next year.

rules.note: Tense in the to-infinitive shifts: 'to be working' (current/ongoing); 'to have done' (past).

examples.plural: People say that technology has changed our lives.
It is widely accepted that technology has transformed modern life. / Technology is widely accepted to have transformed modern life.

rules.note: Reporting passives let you make a claim without naming the source — useful when discussing widely-held views.

sections.tip: Drop 1–2 reporting passives in every Task 2 essay — typically in the introduction ('It is widely believed that…') and conclusion ('It can therefore be argued that…').

sections.title: 4. Causative — Have / Get Something Done
sections.summary: Express that someone else did the action for you. Common in Speaking and useful for Task 2 social-issue essays.

rules.pattern: HAVE + object + V3 — arranging a service

examples.plural: I have my car serviced every year.
I am having my house painted this week.
She had her hair cut yesterday.
I will have my dress dry-cleaned for the wedding.
You should have your eyes checked annually.
I got my car repaired yesterday.
She got her teeth whitened last month.
Where do you usually get your hair cut?

rules.note: 'Get' is more common in spoken English, 'have' in formal/written. Same meaning.

rules.pattern: Negative meaning — experiencing something done to you

examples.plural: He had his wallet stolen on the train.
She got her phone confiscated by the teacher.
Many citizens had their homes destroyed in the flood.

rules.note: Same form, different meaning — context tells you whether it's an arrangement or an unfortunate experience.

examples.singular: Q: Do you cut your own hair?
examples.plural: A: No, I usually have it cut at a salon. / I get my hair cut every two months.

examples.singular: Q: Do you fix things yourself?
examples.plural: A: Some things, but for cars and appliances I prefer to have them repaired by professionals.

rules.note: These answers sound noticeably more advanced than 'No, somebody cuts my hair' (Band 6).

sections.title: 5. Passive in IELTS Writing Task 1 — Process Diagrams
sections.summary: Process diagrams (how something is made / how something works) are typically 70% passive. Mastering this means an instant Band 7+ on Task 1 grammar.

examples.plural: Raw materials are first collected from the source.
The mixture is heated to 200°C and then poured into moulds.
Once filtered, the liquid is bottled and labelled.
The product must be tested before being shipped.
After the components have been assembled, the unit is packaged.

items: Firstly, the raw material is collected.
Subsequently, it is processed in a large machine.
Once heated, the mixture is poured into moulds.
After cooling, the products are inspected for quality.
Finally, they are packaged and distributed to retailers.
rules.note: Connectors for processes: firstly, subsequently, then, once, after which, at this stage, meanwhile, finally.

examples.plural: After the mixture has been heated, it is poured into moulds.
Once heated, the mixture is poured into moulds.
Cooled and inspected, the products are then packaged.

rules.note: Reduced passives ('Once heated…', 'Having been processed…') are exactly what Band 7+ Task 1 looks like.

sections.tip: Pick any IELTS Task 1 process diagram online and write a 150-word description. After writing, count: passive verbs / total verbs. Aim for at least 70% passive in process descriptions.

sections.title: 6. Passive in IELTS Writing Task 2 — Strategic Use
sections.summary: Task 2 should mix active and passive. Reporting passives and modal passives are the high-value moves.

examples.plural: It is widely believed that technology has transformed modern life.
It has often been argued that economic growth comes at an environmental cost.

rules.pattern: Body paragraph — modal passives for recommendations

examples.plural: Stricter regulations must be implemented to address this issue.
More resources should be allocated to public transport.
Significant progress can be achieved through international cooperation.

rules.pattern: Conclusion — modal perfect passive for retrospect

examples.plural: Many of these problems could have been avoided with earlier intervention.
Such measures should have been introduced decades ago.

rules.pattern: When to PREFER active in Task 2

examples.plural: It is felt by many people that…
Many people feel that…

rules.note: If the doer is important, use active. Reserve passive for objective statements.

sections.title: 7. Top Passive-Voice Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: Specific mistakes that pull Band 7 candidates down.

examples.plural: ✔ The bridge WAS built in 1850.
✔ The decision HAS been made. / The decision WAS made.

examples.singular: ❌ The book was wrote by Orwell.
examples.plural: ✔ The book was WRITTEN by Orwell.
✔ The game has been WON.
✔ The cake was EATEN.

rules.note: Memorise the irregular V3 list. The 30 most common irregulars (be/been, do/done, go/gone, give/given, take/taken, see/seen, etc.) cover 90% of usage.

examples.singular: ❌ He was gone home. (gone is intransitive — no object)
examples.plural: ✔ He went home.
✔ The accident happened yesterday. (happen has no passive)

rules.note: Verbs without an object (intransitive) cannot be passive: arrive, happen, go, come, exist, occur, sleep.

rules.pattern: 4. Over-use of passive throughout an essay

examples.singular: ❌ It is felt that it has been argued that it can be said that…
examples.plural: ✔ Mix active and passive. Use passive deliberately, not by default.

examples.singular: ❌ The cake was made with my mother.
examples.plural: ✔ The cake was made BY my mother. (agent = by)

examples.singular: ❌ The cake was made by butter.
examples.plural: ✔ The cake was made WITH butter. (instrument/material = with)

rules.pattern: 6. Reporting passive — wrong tense in to-infinitive

examples.singular: ❌ He is believed to steal the money. (sounds like a habit)
examples.plural: ✔ He is believed to HAVE STOLEN the money. (past act → 'to have + V3')

examples.singular: ❌ She is said to be working there last year.
examples.plural: ✔ She is said to HAVE WORKED there last year.

rules.pattern: 7. Causative — using base verb instead of V3

examples.singular: ❌ I had my hair cutting yesterday.
examples.plural: ✔ I had my hair CUT yesterday. (V3, not -ing)

examples.singular: ❌ She got her car repair last week.
examples.plural: ✔ She got her car REPAIRED last week.
✔ I WAS GIVEN a book. (use subject pronoun)

sections.tip: Print the irregular V3 list (be/been, do/done, see/seen, give/given, take/taken, write/written, eat/eaten, drive/driven, break/broken, choose/chosen, speak/spoken, steal/stolen, etc.). Drill 5 per day until automatic. This single drill fixes 60% of Band 6 passive errors.

checklist: I can conjugate any verb in passive across all 12 tenses without hesitation.
I know all 30 most common irregular past participles (V3) and never write *wrote/*ate/*winned in passive.
I use modal passives (must be addressed, should be implemented, can be achieved) at least once per Task 2 essay.
I use modal perfect passives (could have been prevented, should have been introduced) where appropriate.
I use reporting passives (It is widely believed that… / X is said to have done Y) in introductions and conclusions.
I use causative have/get + V3 ('I had my hair cut') in Speaking, not 'somebody cut my hair'.
I aim for ~70% passive in IELTS Task 1 process diagrams.
I use reduced passive participles ('Once heated, the mixture…', 'Having been informed, she…') in process descriptions.
I never make intransitive verbs passive (gone, happened, occurred).
I use 'by' for the agent (by my mother) and 'with' for the instrument (with a knife) — never the wrong way.
I balance active and passive in Task 2 — never write four passives in a row.
